name: Ralf Tiedje

hook: Yes. So one interesting phenomenon is that cyclin E1 amplified cancers are almost always P16 high, so they express an endogenous inhibitor of CDK4/6. So it appears that, and there's also evidence for
email_subject: Your AACR 2026 work on PKMYT1 — a CrisPRO data angle
schema_a_title: Molecular Glue Degrader Targeting the CCNE1 Protein
schema_a_key_findings: ['MRT55811 is a potent and selective CCNE1 degrader, exclusively active in CCNE1 amplified cell lines.', 'MRT55811 induces co-degradation of CCNE1 and CDK2, leading to Rb dephosphorylation and S-phase

crispro_opportunities.description: While CCNE1 amplification is a strong biomarker, some amplified cell lines show reduced sensitivity, potentially due to P16 expression or other factors.
crispro_opportunities.transcript_evidence: Yes. So one interesting phenomenon is that cyclin E1 amplified cancers are almost always P16 high, so they express an endogenous inhibitor of CDK4/6. So it appears that, and there's also evidence for that, that there is a downside of having both hyperactive CDK2 and active CDK4/6 on top of it. So it's either/or in most cases. There was, I think in this panel, two exceptions, which had low P16, and this explains that then CDK4/6 is active at the same time as CDK2 and pure cyclin E1 degradation doesn't work.
crispro_opportunities.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can analyze the interplay of CCNE1 amplification, P16 expression, and CDK4/6 activity to refine patient stratification for MRT55811.
external_validation_needed: TCGA data analysis: correlation of CCNE1 amplification, P16 expression, and CDK4/6 activity in various cancer types
ClinicalTrials.gov: patient selection criteria for MRT55811 trials

crispro_opportunities.description: The CCNE1 degrader shows activity in models resistant to CDK2 inhibitors, suggesting a potential to overcome resistance to existing therapies.
crispro_opportunities.transcript_evidence: CDK2 inhibitors, interestingly, are a bit different. So they do show preferential activity for cyclin E1 amplified cell lines, and that's more obvious in the lower plot where you can see the GI50s. But there are significant overlaps, so there's also a lot of activity in cell lines that are not amplified. We have seen this with multiple CDK2 inhibitors, not all are shown here.
crispro_opportunities.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can identify patient populations that have failed or are resistant to CDK2 inhibitors but may benefit from CCNE1 degradation.
external_validation_needed: Clinical trial data: response rates of CDK2 inhibitors in CCNE1 amplified cancers
Biomarker analysis: identify patients with CCNE1 amplification who are refractory to CDK2 inhibitors

crispro_opportunities.transcript_evidence: Speaker discussed that T‑cell engager alone yields limited responses in immune‑desert tumors
crispro_opportunities.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can simulate triple‑combination dynamics and predict synergistic windows

name: Junyoung Wang
crispro_angle: CrisPRO can identify which AACR 2026 abstracts address MHC-I loss as immunotherapy resistance — NAT10-MYC axis is a novel resistance mechanism that hapten-based vaccines may bypass
hook: Speaker said: "NAT10-MYC loop drives autophagy-dependent MHC-I downregulation and immunotherapy resistance in CRC"
email_subject: NAT10-MYC / MHC-I loss in CRC — CrisPRO resistance mechanism mapping
stc1010_relevance: MHC-I-independent killing via hapten may differentiate STC-1010 from T-cell-dependent immunotherapies
schema_a_title: NAT10‑MYC loop drives autophagy‑dependent MHC‑I downregulation and immunotherapy resistance in MSS colorectal cancer

crispro_opportunities.description: Develop next‑generation mRNA TCEs targeting CD20 or CD22 for CD19‑negative relapses
crispro_opportunities.transcript_evidence: Audience asked about CD19‑negative patients and alternative targets
crispro_opportunities.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can prioritize alternative antigen‑binding domains based on tumor expression atlases

crispro_opportunities.description: Create a predictive model of CD19 density and prior anti‑CD19 therapy to select responders
crispro_opportunities.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted all patients were CD19‑positive but did not stratify further

name: Robert Seder

crispro_opportunities.description: DCAF5 loss restores SWI/SNF complex activity in SMARCB1‑deficient tumors, representing a synthetic‑lethal vulnerability that CrisPRO could model to predict responders.
crispro_opportunities.transcript_evidence: Speaker said: "When we knock out DCAF5, we see the SwitchNif complex accumulating... transcription is rescued... tumors were completely wiped out and did not recur."
crispro_opportunities.crispro_angle: CrisPRO can integrate CRISPR dependency data with epigenomic signatures to prioritize patients whose tumors show SMARCB1 loss and DCAF5 dependency, enabling biomarker‑driven trial design.

crispro_opportunities.description: No biomarker was presented to identify which SMARCB1‑deficient cancers are most dependent on DCAF5, an opening for CrisPRO to develop a predictive expression signature.
crispro_opportunities.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that DCAF5 dependency was observed in rhabdoid tumor cell lines but did not discuss patient‑level biomarkers.
crispro_opportunities.crispro_angle: Use machine‑learning on DepMap and patient transcriptomics to derive a DCAF5‑dependency score for trial enrollment.
